title: Classify Data as Numerical or Categorical
description: Determine whether a data set consists of numerical measurements or categories.
condition: A data set description is given (e.g., hand widths, eye colors).
response: State whether the data are numerical (measurements/counts) or categorical (labels/groups).
label: Concept
example: "Students’ hand widths" → Numerical; "Students’ eye colors" → Categorical.
notes: Based on definitions of data types.

title: Compare Medians on Box Plots
description: Determine which of two box plots has the greater median by comparing the positions of the median lines.
condition: Given two box plots.
response: Identify the distribution whose median line is farther to the right as having the greater median.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Team A’s median is at 18 and Team B’s median is at 10, then Team A has the greater median.

title: Compare Minimum or Maximum Values
description: Identify which distribution has the greater minimum or maximum by comparing whisker endpoints.
condition: Given two box plots and asked which has the greater minimum or maximum.
response: Select the distribution whose whisker endpoint is farther to the right for maximum or left for minimum as appropriate.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Team A’s maximum is 32 and Team B’s maximum is 28, then Team A has the greater maximum.

title: Compare Ranges on Box Plots
description: Determine which of two box plots has the greater range by comparing minimum and maximum whiskers.
condition: Given two box plots.
response: Compute or compare (maximum − minimum) for each and select the greater value.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Team A ranges from 2 to 32 (range 30) and Team B from 4 to 28 (range 24), Team A has the greater range.

title: Compare Value to Mean Using MAD
description: Compare a specific data value to a distribution by reasoning about its distance from the mean relative to the mean absolute deviation.
condition: Given a mean, a mean absolute deviation, and a specific data value.
response: Determine in which distribution the value is relatively farther below or above the mean based on the MAD.
label: Principle
example: Race 1: mean = 58, MAD = 14 → one MAD below mean is $58-14=44$. Race 2: mean = 55, MAD = 3 → one MAD below mean is $55-3=52$. A time of 48 minutes is farther below the mean in Race 2 relative to its MAD.
notes: Uses MAD as a measure of variability for comparison.

title: Compare Variability Between Distributions
description: Determine which distribution has greater or lesser variability based on spread in graphs.
condition: Given two graphical distributions.
response: Identify which distribution has data values that vary more or less.
label: Production / Skill
example: If Practice B data are clustered more tightly than Practice A, conclude Practice B varies less.

title: Compute Absolute Deviation from the Mean
description: Find the absolute deviation of a single data value from the mean by taking the absolute value of their difference.
condition: Given a data value x and the mean μ of the data set.
response: Compute |x − μ|.
label: Principle / Model
example: If μ = 45 and x = 48, then the absolute deviation is |48 - 45| = 3.

title: Compute Interquartile Range
description: Find the interquartile range by subtracting the first quartile from the third quartile.
condition: Given Q1 and Q3 for a data set.
response: Compute $\text{IQR} = Q3 - Q1$.
label: Principle
example: If $Q1=58$ and $Q3=60$, then $\text{IQR}=60-58=2$.
notes: Requires prior identification of quartiles. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compute Mean Absolute Deviation
description: Find the mean absolute deviation by averaging the absolute deviations of each data value from the mean.
condition: Given a data set and its mean.
response: Compute each |x − μ| and then find their mean.
label: Principle / Model
example: For data 6, 8 with mean 7: deviations are |6-7|=1 and |8-7|=1, so MAD = \(\frac{1+1}{2} = 1\).

title: Compute Mean from Data Set
description: Calculate the mean by summing all data values and dividing by the number of values.
condition: Given a finite numerical data set.
response: Add all values and divide by the number of values.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compute Range of a Data Set
description: Determine the range by subtracting the minimum value from the maximum value.
condition: Given a set of numerical data values.
response: Compute max − min.
label: Principle / Model
example: For data 3, 4, 8: range = 8 - 3 = 5.

title: Compute Total Count from Histogram
description: Find the total number of data points by summing all bin frequencies in a histogram.
condition: A histogram displays frequencies for all intervals and the question asks for total number of observations.
response: Add all bin frequencies to obtain the total count.
label: Principle / Model
example: If bin frequencies are 10, 7, 5, 4, 3, 6, then total is $10+7+5+4+3+6=35$.
notes: Each data point is counted in exactly one bin.

title: Construct Histogram from Frequency Table
description: Create a histogram by representing each class interval with a bar whose height equals its frequency.
condition: A grouped frequency table with class intervals and tallies is provided.
response: Draw contiguous bars over each interval with heights equal to the corresponding frequencies.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 100 to <110 has frequency 8, draw a bar over $[100,110)$ with height 8.
notes: Bars must touch because intervals are continuous.

title: Count Data Points from Display
description: Determine the total number of data values by counting marks in a dot plot or tally table.
condition: Given a dot plot or tally chart representing data values.
response: Count all dots or tally marks to find the total number of data points.
label: Category
example: If a dot plot shows 35 dots, then there are 35 data values (students).
notes: Response is a single total for a given display.

title: Create Box Plot
description: Construct a box plot using the minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum.
condition: Given the five-number summary of a data set.
response: Draw a number line, mark min and max as whiskers, draw a box from Q1 to Q3, and mark the median inside the box.
label: Production / Skill
example: If min=17, Q1=18, median=19, Q3=20, max=22, draw whiskers at 17 and 22 and a box from 18 to 20 with a line at 19.

title: Extract Data Values from a Dot Plot
description: Translate a dot plot into a list of numerical data values by counting each dot at its labeled position.
condition: Given a dot plot with dots aligned above labeled numerical values.
response: List each value once for every dot shown at that position.
label: Production / Skill
example: If three dots are above 6 and two above 8, the data include 6, 6, 6, 8, 8.

title: Identify a Gap in a Dot Plot
description: Recognize an interval on a dot plot where no data values occur.
condition: Given a dot plot with values marked along a number line.
response: State that a gap exists between two values if no dots appear in that interval.
label: Category
example: If dots appear at 4 and 7 but none at 5 or 6, there is a gap from 4 to 7.

title: Identify a Statistical Question
description: Determine whether a question anticipates variability in data and requires collecting data from multiple individuals.
condition: A question is posed about a quantity or characteristic.
response: State that it is statistical if it expects variability in responses; otherwise not statistical.
label: Concept
example: "How many minutes does a student typically listen to music in a day?" → Statistical (answers vary).
notes: Key feature: anticipates variability across individuals.

title: Identify Approximate Center from Histogram
description: Determine the interval that represents the approximate center (typical value) of a distribution.
condition: A histogram is shown and asked which interval contains the center.
response: Select the interval where the distribution clusters most centrally (often highest middle bar).
label: Rule / Plan
example: If the tallest central bar is 8 to <12, state the center is in $8\text{ to }<12$.

title: Identify Approximately Symmetric Distribution
description: Recognize a distribution where left and right sides are roughly mirror images around the center.
condition: A dot plot or histogram shows similar spread and frequencies on both sides of a central value.
response: Classify the distribution as approximately symmetric.
label: Concept
example: If frequencies decrease similarly on both sides of 10, the distribution is approximately symmetric.
notes: Visual judgment; no exact calculation required.

title: Identify Distribution Shape
description: Classify a distribution as approximately symmetric, skewed left, or skewed right based on tail direction.
condition: Given a graph of a numerical distribution.
response: State whether the distribution is symmetric, skewed left, or skewed right.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If the right tail is longer, classify the distribution as skewed right.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify Largest Gap in Data
description: Determine the interval between consecutive data values that has no observations and is widest.
condition: Given a plotted or listed data set.
response: Locate and state the interval with the greatest distance and no data points.
label: Production / Skill
example: If no values occur between 28 and 37 and this is the widest empty interval, state 28 to 37.

title: Identify Median from Ordered Data
description: Determine the median by locating the middle value of an ordered data set.
condition: Given an ordered list or dot plot with an odd number of data points.
response: Select the middle value (\(\frac{n+1}{2}\)th value) as the median.
label: Concept
example: For 13 values, the median is the 7th value in order; if the 7th value is 21, the median is 21.

title: Identify Peak of Distribution
description: Find the data value with the highest frequency in a distribution.
condition: Given a frequency display (dot plot, histogram, or table).
response: State the value with the greatest frequency.
label: Production / Skill
example: If 3 occurs most often, the peak is at 3.

title: Identify Skewness Direction
description: Determine whether a distribution is skewed left or right based on the direction of its tail.
condition: A dot plot or histogram shows a longer tail on one side.
response: Classify as skewed right (tail to the right) or skewed left (tail to the left).
label: Concept
example: If most data cluster at low values with a long tail to higher values, the distribution is skewed right.
notes: Right skew = tail extends to larger values.

title: Interpret Mean Absolute Deviation
description: Compare a specific value to a distribution using the mean and mean absolute deviation.
condition: Given a mean, a mean absolute deviation, and a specific data value.
response: Compare how many MADs the value is above or below the mean to judge relative standing.
label: Principle / Model
example: If mean $=46$ and MAD $=2$, one MAD above is $48$. A value of $51$ is more than one MAD above the mean.

title: Interpret Mean as Equal Share
description: Understand the mean as the value each data point would have if the total were distributed equally.
condition: Asked to interpret the meaning of the mean in context.
response: State that the mean represents the equal amount each would have if shared evenly.
label: Concept
example: If 52 oranges were eaten over 4 weeks, the mean 13 means 13 oranges per week if shared equally.

title: Interpret Meaning of Interquartile Range
description: Recognize that the interquartile range represents the spread of the middle 50% of the data.
condition: Asked what the interquartile range represents.
response: State that it is the range of the middle 50% of the data.
label: Concept
example: The interquartile range describes the spread of the middle 50% of the values.

title: Interpret Median as Measure of Center
description: Recognize the median as a measure of center and that about half the data are at or above it.
condition: Asked to interpret the meaning of the median in context.
response: State that the median is a measure of center and about half the data are at least the median.
label: Concept
example: If the median number of games is 21, then about half the matches had at least 21 games.

title: Interpret Quartiles as Percentages
description: Use quartiles in a box plot to determine approximate percentages of data above or below a value.
condition: Given a box plot and asked about the percent of data at or below/above a quartile or median.
response: Use the fact that each quartile represents about 25% of the data to determine the percentage.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 60 minutes is the first quartile (Q1), then about 25% of students practiced 60 minutes or less.

title: Match Box Plot to Dot Plot
description: Select the box plot whose five-number summary matches the data shown in a dot plot.
condition: Given a dot plot and multiple box plot options.
response: Determine the five-number summary from the dot plot and choose the box plot with matching min, Q1, median, Q3, and max.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a dot plot has min 1, Q1 2, median 3, Q3 7, max 9, select the box plot displaying those values.

title: Match Measure of Center/Spread to Skew
description: Select appropriate measures of center and variability based on distribution shape.
condition: Given a skewed distribution.
response: State that the median and interquartile range are more appropriate than the mean and MAD.
label: Principle / Model
example: For a right-skewed distribution, use the median and IQR.

title: Order Data Sets by Mean Absolute Deviation
description: Compare mean absolute deviations to determine relative variability.
condition: Given two or more data sets with known or computed MADs.
response: Order from smallest MAD (least variability) to largest MAD (greatest variability).
label: Principle / Model
example: If MADs are 1.2, 1.5, and 2.0, order: 1.2 < 1.5 < 2.0 (least to greatest variability).

title: Order Distributions by Interquartile Range
description: Compare multiple data displays by estimating and ordering their interquartile ranges (IQRs).
condition: Given two or more data distributions (e.g., dot plots) and asked to order them by least to greatest interquartile range.
response: Estimate each distribution’s IQR (Q3 − Q1) and rank them from smallest to largest.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Distribution A has Q1 = 12 and Q3 = 14 (IQR = 2), and Distribution B has Q1 = 10 and Q3 = 16 (IQR = 6), then A has the smaller IQR.

title: Recognize Outlier in Histogram
description: Identify when a distribution includes a value far from the rest of the data (an outlier) based on a visual display.
condition: Given a histogram and asked whether the distribution has an outlier.
response: Identify a bar or value far separated from the main cluster as an outlier.
label: Production / Skill
example: If most wins are between 80 and 100 but one season shows 55 wins far from the rest, identify 55 as an outlier.
